hook: A Byzantine cathedral of gold mosaic behind a plain Bayswater street.
facts: Built 1877 to 1879 for London's Greek merchant colony, in Byzantine-revival style by John Oldrid Scott.
The interior was later smothered in glittering mosaic, with later work by the artist Boris Anrep.
It serves as a cathedral of the Greek Orthodox church in Britain.
The poet George Seferis, a Greek diplomat in London, worshipped here.
w: Behind a plain Bayswater street front opens a blaze of gold: a Byzantine-revival cathedral raised by London's Greek merchant colony in 1879, its dome and apse smothered in glittering mosaic, some by Boris Anrep. The poet Seferis worshipped here; the bells still ring for the diaspora. Constantinople, off the Moscow Road.

hook: London's wildest Arts and Crafts church, hidden behind a plain brick front.
facts: Built 1884 to 1887 by Hugh Roumieu Gough as an Anglo-Catholic stronghold.
The interior holds a towering gilded reredos, beaten-metal Art Nouveau fittings by Bainbridge Reynolds and a sgraffito frieze.
In 1898 anti-ritualist protesters stormed the church and smashed fittings.
Home to the Society of the Faith.
w: From outside, a sober brick barn; inside, the wildest Arts and Crafts church in London. Anglo-Catholic zealots filled St Cuthbert's from 1887 with a towering gilded reredos, beaten-metal Art Nouveau fittings and a sgraffito frieze. Anti-ritualists once stormed in and smashed the place up. Riotous faith behind a plain face.
